name: Emergence of Leaders I: Gandhi, Nehru, Bose, Ambedkar

sourceRefs.label: 2024_q page 4 Q2 (Ambedkar's 'Problem of the Rupee' thesis, with answer key)
2024_q page 85 Q173-174 (Nehru quote; Bose's birth date, with answer key)
2022_q1 page 24 Q133 (Bose founded Forward Bloc, with answer key)
2025_q1 page 118 Q183 (Gandhi on Thirukkural, with answer key)
2025_q page 96 Q100 (Objective Resolution authorship — Nehru, not Ambedkar, with answer key)
standard biographical milestones (movements led, key roles) — well-established, no direct past-paper hit needed for these specific facts

mkgandhi.org, Wikipedia, drambedkarbooks.com - Phoenix Settlement/Indian Opinion, Tolstoy Farm, Hind Swaraj, Ambedkar's newspapers (Mooknayak through Prabuddha Bharat), his 1956 Buddhism conversion at Deekshabhoomi, Second Round Table Conference/Lord Willingdon, Nehru's prison-written books

Wikipedia, vajiramandravi.com - Poona Pact, Mahad Satyagraha, Ambedkar's pre-independence parties and Law Ministership, Azad Hind Government/Rani of Jhansi Regiment/INA Red Fort Trials, Bose's Taihoku death controversy, the Bose-vs-Tagore Gandhi-title distinction, Panchsheel/Non-Aligned Movement

researchNotes: The 'Emergence of leaders' syllabus clause names ~12 individuals — split into two topics for manageable daily depth. This first topic covers the 4 most nationally-prominent figures (Gandhi, Nehru, Bose, Ambedkar); a second topic will cover the more Tamil-Nadu-specific and remaining names (Bhagat Singh, Bharathiar, V.O.Chidambaranar, Kamarajar, Periyar, Rajaji, Azad, Tagore). Strong real evidence — 5 confirmed hits across 2022/2024/2025, including a genuine authorship trap (the Objective Resolution is commonly mis-attributed to Ambedkar in distractor options, but it was actually Nehru who moved/authored it in the Constituent Assembly). Expanded 2026-07 pass: re-scanned OCR (a 2022_q1 page-025 hit about a 1945 'one-man commission on Netaji's disappearance' was found but NOT used — the year/name pairing looked internally inconsistent with known history of the Khosla Commission, and re-verifying precisely would have taken too long relative to its value, so skipped per the 'skip rather than guess' rule). Added 7 more high-yield biographical facts via web research: Gandhi's South African communes (Phoenix Settlement/Indian Opinion, Tolstoy Farm) and his own key books, Ambedkar's full newspaper lineage and Buddhism conversion, the Second Round Table Conference detail, and Nehru's prison-written books — rounding out coverage beyond the original pass's thesis-titles-and-quotes lean. Round-2 pass (2026-07, second expansion): checked for overlap with adjacent topics first (a3-satyagraha-militant-movements, a3-current-affairs-5 — neither covers Poona Pact/Mahad Satyagraha/Azad Hind Government/INA trials/Panchsheel/NAM in depth, so added here without duplication). New additions: Poona Pact and Mahad Satyagraha (Ambedkar's pre-constitutional activism), his pre-independence parties and Law Ministership, the Azad Hind Government/Rani of Jhansi Regiment/INA Red Fort Trials/Taihoku death-controversy cluster for Bose, the lesser-known Bose-not-Tagore 'Father of the Nation' attribution, and Nehru's Panchsheel/NAM foreign-policy legacy. Roughly doubled mock questions (17→27); deliberately stopped short of covering the Simon Commission/Nehru Report/Cripps Mission in depth since those sit more naturally in the adjacent INC and satyagraha-movements topic files.

reading.intro: First of two 'Emergence of Leaders' topics. Real exam evidence favors specific, checkable facts (thesis titles, exact dates, party foundings, direct quotes) over general biography — and includes at least one genuine authorship trap to watch for.

qa.question: Real exam: What was the title of Dr. B.R. Ambedkar's economics thesis?
qa.answer: "The Problem of the Rupee" (his doctoral thesis, examining India's currency/monetary system under British rule).

qa.question: Real exam (trap): Who actually authored/moved the 'Objective Resolution' in the Constituent Assembly — is it Dr. B.R. Ambedkar?
qa.answer: No — it was Jawaharlal Nehru, not Ambedkar, who moved the Objective Resolution (adopted 22 January 1947), which later became the basis for the Constitution's Preamble. Ambedkar's major constitutional role was instead as Chairman of the Drafting Committee.

qa.question: Real exam: Who said, 'Great Leaders have something in them which inspires a whole people and makes them do great deeds'?
qa.answer: Jawaharlal Nehru.

qa.question: Real exam: When was Subhas Chandra Bose born, and which party did he found?
qa.answer: 23 January 1897. He founded the Forward Bloc Party (a left-leaning party within/breaking from Congress).

qa.question: Real exam: What did Mahatma Gandhi call the Thirukkural?
qa.answer: He referred to it as 'Tamil's sacred book' (echoing similar reverence expressed by other figures like Bishop Caldwell about Tamil literature's civilizational depth).

qa.question: What were Gandhi's major movements, in order?
qa.answer: Champaran Satyagraha (1917, his first major movement in India) → Non-Cooperation Movement (1920-22) → Civil Disobedience Movement / Dandi Salt March (1930) → Quit India Movement (1942).

qa.question: What was Nehru's role after independence, and what is his most famous speech called?
qa.answer: India's first Prime Minister (1947-1964). His most famous speech, delivered on the eve of independence (14-15 August 1947), is known as 'Tryst with Destiny'.

qa.question: What was Subhas Chandra Bose's most famous call to action, and what military force did he lead?
qa.answer: "Give me blood, and I will give you freedom" (Tum Mujhe Khoon Do, Main Tumhe Azadi Doonga). He led the Indian National Army (INA / Azad Hind Fauj).

qa.question: What was Ambedkar's principal constitutional role, and what else is he known for?
qa.answer: Chairman of the Constitution's Drafting Committee — often called the 'Father/Architect of the Indian Constitution'. He's also known for leading the Dalit rights movement and (later in life) his conversion to Buddhism.

qa.question: What was the Phoenix Settlement, and what newspaper did Gandhi run from it?
qa.answer: Gandhi's first intentional community/commune, founded in 1904 near Durban, South Africa — built around ideals of simplicity, self-sufficiency, and communal living. From there he published Indian Opinion, a weekly newspaper promoting satyagraha and Indian rights in South Africa.

qa.question: What was Tolstoy Farm, and why that name?
qa.answer: Gandhi's second South African commune, established in 1910 near Johannesburg (Transvaal), serving as the headquarters for the satyagraha campaign against discriminatory laws targeting Indians there. It was named after the Russian writer Leo Tolstoy, whose book 'The Kingdom of God Is Within You' deeply influenced Gandhi's philosophy of nonviolence.

qa.question: Name two of Gandhi's own foundational books, and briefly what each is about.
qa.answer: 'Hind Swaraj' (1909) — a short political pamphlet setting out his critique of modern Western civilisation and his vision of self-rule. 'The Story of My Experiments with Truth' — his autobiography (serialized 1925-29), describing his personal and moral development.

qa.question: What were the successive newspapers/journals B.R. Ambedkar founded and edited over his career?
qa.answer: Mooknayak ('Leader of the Voiceless', Marathi fortnightly, launched 31 January 1920, funded by Chhatrapati Shahu of the Kolhapur princely state) → Bahishkrit Bharat (1927) → Samata (1928) → Janata (1930) → renamed Prabuddha Bharat (from 1956) — spanning most of his public life.

qa.question: When and where did Dr. B.R. Ambedkar formally convert to Buddhism, and what happened shortly after?
qa.answer: On 14 October 1956, at Deekshabhoomi in Nagpur, taking the three refuges and five precepts from the Buddhist monk Bhadant U Chandramani, then administering them to hundreds of thousands of his followers present. He died less than two months later, on 6 December 1956.

qa.question: Which Round Table Conference did Gandhi personally attend as the sole INC representative, and who was the Viceroy of India at the time?
qa.answer: The Second Round Table Conference (London, 1931) — Gandhi attended on behalf of the Indian National Congress (following the Gandhi-Irwin Pact). The Viceroy at the time was Lord Willingdon, who had succeeded Lord Irwin in April 1931.

qa.question: Name two of Nehru's own major books, and under what circumstance was much of his writing done?
qa.answer: 'Discovery of India' and 'Glimpses of World History' — both substantially written during his long periods of imprisonment by the British, a mark of how Nehru used his years in jail for scholarly writing rather than idle time.

qa.question: Round-2 pass — What was the Poona Pact (1932), and what did it replace?
qa.answer: An agreement between Gandhi and Ambedkar signed in September 1932, after Gandhi undertook a fast-unto-death in Yerwada jail to oppose the British Communal Award's provision of SEPARATE electorates for the 'Depressed Classes' (Dalits). The Poona Pact replaced separate electorates with a system of RESERVED seats for Depressed Classes within the general electorate — a compromise Ambedkar accepted under pressure, though he remained critical of it in later years.

qa.question: What was the Mahad Satyagraha (1927), and what did it assert?
qa.answer: Led by Dr. B.R. Ambedkar at Mahad (Maharashtra) in 1927, it asserted the right of 'untouchables' to draw water from the public Chavdar Tank, previously denied to them despite it being legally open to all — an early and symbolically important direct-action campaign in Ambedkar's fight against untouchability, distinct from and predating his later constitutional role.

qa.question: What political parties did Ambedkar found before independence?
qa.answer: The Independent Labour Party (1936) and later the All India Scheduled Castes Federation (1942) — both vehicles for organized Dalit political representation, prior to his post-independence role as Law Minister and Drafting Committee chairman.

qa.question: What government position did Ambedkar hold in independent India's first cabinet?
qa.answer: India's first Law Minister (1947-1951), in addition to chairing the Constitution's Drafting Committee.

qa.question: What was the Azad Hind Government, when/where was it declared, and what did it do?
qa.answer: The Provisional Government of Free India (Azad Hind), proclaimed by Subhas Chandra Bose in Singapore on 21 October 1943, functioning as a government-in-exile with its own currency, court, and civil code, aligned with Japan during WWII. It formally declared war on Britain and the United States.

qa.question: What was the Rani of Jhansi Regiment?
qa.answer: The Indian National Army's all-women combat regiment, formed under Bose's Azad Hind movement and led by Captain Lakshmi Sahgal — named after the 1857 warrior-queen Rani Lakshmibai of Jhansi, and notable as one of the earliest all-women combat units in Asia.

qa.question: What were the INA (Red Fort) Trials of 1945-46, and why did they matter politically?
qa.answer: British India put three senior INA officers — Shah Nawaz Khan, Gurbaksh Singh Dhillon, and Prem Sahgal (a Hindu, a Sikh, and a Muslim, deliberately drawing cross-community support) — on trial for treason at the Red Fort, Delhi. The trials triggered massive nationwide public sympathy and protest (including mutiny sentiment in the armed forces), significantly pressuring the British toward the decision to leave India.

qa.question: What is the officially accepted account of Subhas Chandra Bose's death, and why does controversy persist?
qa.answer: He is officially reported to have died on 18 August 1945 in a plane crash at Taihoku (Taipei), Taiwan, while flying to Tokyo after Japan's WWII surrender. Controversy persists because no body was conclusively identified at the time, prompting multiple later Indian government inquiries (the Shah Nawaz Committee, the Khosla Commission, and the Mukherjee Commission) — of which only the Mukherjee Commission (2005) rejected the plane-crash theory as unproven, though this finding was not accepted by the Government of India.

qa.question: When was Gandhi assassinated, by whom, and what title did Subhas Chandra Bose (not Tagore) give him, and in what context?
qa.answer: Gandhi was assassinated on 30 January 1948 by Nathuram Godse. While Rabindranath Tagore is widely credited with popularizing the title 'Mahatma' ('Great Soul') for Gandhi, it is Subhas Chandra Bose who is credited with first publicly calling Gandhi the 'Father of the Nation', in a 1944 radio address from Rangoon via Azad Hind Radio — an important, less commonly known distinction from the 'Mahatma' title's separate origin.

qa.question: What are Panchsheel and the Non-Aligned Movement, both associated with Nehru's foreign policy?
qa.answer: Panchsheel ('Five Principles of Peaceful Coexistence') was a set of principles for international relations jointly agreed by Nehru and Chinese Premier Zhou Enlai in 1954. The Non-Aligned Movement (NAM), formally founded at the 1961 Belgrade conference with Nehru as a key co-founder alongside Yugoslavia's Tito, Egypt's Nasser, and Indonesia's Sukarno, committed member states to avoid formal alignment with either Cold War bloc (US or USSR).

questions.prompt: Dr. B.R. Ambedkar's doctoral thesis was titled:

questions.explanation: Jawaharlal Nehru — not Ambedkar, a common trap.

questions.prompt: Gandhi's Phoenix Settlement, his first intentional community, was founded in 1904 near which city?
From the Phoenix Settlement, Gandhi published which weekly newspaper?

questions.explanation: Leo Tolstoy, whose 'The Kingdom of God Is Within You' influenced Gandhi's nonviolence philosophy.

questions.prompt: Gandhi's short but foundational 1909 political pamphlet was titled:
B.R. Ambedkar's first newspaper, a Marathi fortnightly launched on 31 January 1920, was called:
Which princely-state ruler provided initial funding for Ambedkar's Mooknayak newspaper?
Dr. B.R. Ambedkar publicly converted to Buddhism on 14 October 1956 at:
Gandhi attended which Round Table Conference (1931) as the sole representative of the INC, held under Viceroy Lord Willingdon?
questions.explanation: The Second Round Table Conference, London, 1931.

questions.prompt: Nehru's 'Discovery of India' and 'Glimpses of World History' were substantially written:
options: During his years as Prime Minister
questions.explanation: During his long periods of imprisonment by the British.

questions.prompt: The Poona Pact (1932) between Gandhi and Ambedkar replaced separate electorates for the Depressed Classes with:
options: Reserved seats within the general electorate
questions.explanation: Reserved seats within the general electorate, following Gandhi's fast-unto-death against the Communal Award's separate-electorate provision.

questions.prompt: The Mahad Satyagraha (1927), led by Ambedkar, asserted the right of 'untouchables' to:
options: Draw water from the public Chavdar Tank
questions.explanation: The right to draw water from the Chavdar Tank at Mahad, Maharashtra.

questions.prompt: Before independence, Ambedkar founded which political party in 1936, later replaced by the Scheduled Castes Federation (1942)?
In independent India's first cabinet, Dr. B.R. Ambedkar served as:
Subhas Chandra Bose proclaimed the Provisional Government of Free India (Azad Hind) in which city, in October 1943?
The Rani of Jhansi Regiment, the INA's all-women combat regiment, was led by:
The 1945-46 INA (Red Fort) Trials tried three officers — a Hindu, a Sikh, and a Muslim — deliberately to draw cross-community support. Who were they?
options: Shah Nawaz Khan, Gurbaksh Singh Dhillon, and Prem Sahgal
Batukeshwar Dutt, Chandrashekhar Azad, and Ashfaqulla Khan
Ram Prasad Bismil, Roshan Singh, and Rajendra Lahiri
questions.explanation: Shah Nawaz Khan, Gurbaksh Singh Dhillon, and Prem Sahgal — the trials sparked massive nationwide sympathy for the INA.

questions.prompt: Subhas Chandra Bose is officially reported to have died on 18 August 1945 due to:
options: A plane crash at Taihoku (Taipei), Taiwan
questions.explanation: A plane crash at Taihoku while flying to Tokyo after Japan's WWII surrender — though controversy over this account persists, examined by later commissions.

questions.prompt: Which leader is credited with first publicly calling Gandhi the 'Father of the Nation', in a 1944 Azad Hind Radio address — distinct from Tagore's separate 'Mahatma' title?
questions.explanation: Subhas Chandra Bose, in a 1944 radio address from Rangoon.
questions.trick: Two separate Gandhi titles, two separate originators: 'Mahatma' is credited to Tagore; 'Father of the Nation' is credited to Bose (1944) — don't merge the two into one attribution.

questions.prompt: Panchsheel (1954) and the Non-Aligned Movement (1961) both reflect which aspect of Nehru's legacy?
options: Foreign policy of peaceful coexistence and Cold War non-alignment
questions.explanation: Foreign policy — Panchsheel (with China, 1954) and NAM co-founding (Belgrade, 1961, with Tito/Nasser/Sukarno).
